speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], interview with Waclaw Kolodziejek, July 18th, 1996. You were tell-- starting to tell me that one day you had a transport that came into Auschwitz, and you were not allowed out of your barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah. They brought a convoy about-- only-- only men, Polish Jew, Polish-Jewish people. I don't know from-- from where, from Warsaw or from Cracow. I-- I believe. Maybe from prison, Pawiak, from Warsaw. That familiar to you, Pawiak? Or maybe from [GERMAN], that's the Gestapo prisoner. And they brought to Auschwitz, told them undress, naked. There come a convoy, soldier with the rifle. He told them, those people, to run in different direction. And the-- there, SS shoot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: As they came off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, by the-- that's why-- that's why the whole reason to ask for them not to look, lay down on the-- on the floor. But I was looking. I see what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen once or many times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many times. And there was also-- made it from Polish-Jewish, they're calling [GERMAN], short. [GERMAN], the German call it, [GERMAN]. [NON-ENGLISH]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get it. I-- I will write it down. We'll get it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- they put on the-- on the-- they used to leave those people, Polish-Jewish people, live in the block 11. And they'd been working very hard and very-- treated very bad. Many of them been killed on the work, on the job. Many people been killed in the block 11, or many people had been executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was happening at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in a different barrack with-- and at that point, you were never with the Jews again. You were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the time, it's-- I was not even thinking. You know, I was live a-- I was-- I feel like-- like I'm an animal, not human. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how-- Most of the people in your barrack were already feeling that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was just-- when I see those people, when they went to Auschwitz, I say, what-- I say to myself, what kind of people there are, you see? That, to me, I said, it looks like a zoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were still working at this time. They were taking you out on the work detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were tearing down these houses. Did you do any other work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- because I-- after I-- I escape from commando to commando, from job to job. Then when they caught me, they whip me. Well, I'm-- I'm in Poland because the job was too-- too hard for me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I try-- I try to do anything to-- to work easy. But there was no easy. And they whipped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got-- do you have scars from your whipping? I mean, do they-- do they really leave scars on your skin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hands, you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened more than once. Did you finally find another job that you felt you could do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick lately. There were still-- there was, I believe, 1941 or '42, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put me on the river. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I-- I-- I-- change the time-- I jumped from-- there was impossible to describe everything already-- already been done to people, too, done to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about some of the things that were happening to you? This, you're-- we're talking now. We're past 1940. We're into 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. [INAUDIBLE] I-- I-- I want to be-- say about my dear beautiful [INAUDIBLE], woman. And the bullet holes are through the-- through the-- four? I believe four trucks. SS brought inside to the Auschwitz, to the block 11, where they were being executed, Polish women, Polish-Jewish women, Polish-- 18, 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: A truckful of just young women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, three, I believe four or six trucks, loaded. And when trucks went through-- through the Auschwitz, for the execution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did they execute the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the living block. They call the living block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was near the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. They brought-- the convoy brought in inside, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were allowed to see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes Because you know, we've been working, make believe, you know, not to look at that but [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen almost every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. Every day, brought fresh transport, segregated, on the front of the gas chamber crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: They segregated people in front of the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what was the process? Describe what they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe also younger people, put to-- because the German knew they were labor work. All the people, mostly going to the gas chamber or executed. Younger people, they're keeping in a-- in a camp for-- for their working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by now, by 1941, there was a gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, there was only one crematorium, I believe, in it--

speaker: interviewer
text: One--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: One crematorium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they took the young people to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do with the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other people, I believe they-- they put in the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had the gas chambers already built?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. I'm sure there were already built.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who worked in these gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to build this gas chamber, there were everybody, prisoner, like me, which I [INAUDIBLE]. But it's everybody, Polish-Jewish, Polish people, German, German prisoner. Because there was many German prisoners in the camp too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you all worked in building these gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many gas chambers were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe there was four in-- in Auschwitz. Two in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you-- were you also sent to Birkenau, to-- to build them? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- we been-- I-- I-- we been in the barracks. I've been working in these barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was still in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- I believe I was transferred from Auschwitz to Birkenau, I believe 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: 19-- so till '42. You were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, have we covered all the things that you worked at in Auschwitz? What other things did you do in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work in the [GERMAN], chopping the wood. Which also is the work, we're sawing with the saw. That's why I-- they-- they-- well, now I was stuck in with a friend of mine, when they caught me. And then that's why I got 50 whips. And that's 100 whips because two-- two whips, they counted as one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk-- explain what they did to you, how they-- how they did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after appell, everybody, they counted the [GERMAN]. Each-- each block, 1,050-- 1,200 people. They belong one SS man, they're calling-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have to be able to say-- one like a-- like an Oberscharfuhrer. [INAUDIBLE] black in-- in appell. Appell, that mean counted how many people. Than after appell, they call my number for the-- for the punishment, for the whips.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they find your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very easy, with the number. And the-- and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And And who saw it? Who decided?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe one of the SS men. I-- I-- SS men-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] could be Oberscharfuhrer, the Sarge. Or [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. That's mean like a colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he decide that you needed to be punished? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just because I was talking to a friend of mine on the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took your number down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after appell, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: After appell they call my number. So I have this-- I-- I go forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a-- the whips was made from a bull. You know, they were the same, with the two, three strokes to the skin, broken. Then I have to count it. [GERMAN], two whips for [GERMAN]. [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. If you make me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Start again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Start, yeah. So I-- I-- I was very strong. But many people got whips too, 20. And they bled throughout-- from out here. And the person died. I was so strong. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had 50 whips. You had really, 100?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- like-- like 100. Because two-- two whips, they were counted as one. Told me 50 whips. If you counted-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Correctly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- like correctly, that's it, 100 whips.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were-- was there a hospital somewhere that they could take care of your skin afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I've been-- and the next day I-- I went to work. No-- no forgettings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any infections from the skin? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the skin was black. And no, there's nothing, nothing. No-- no care.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so that's another instance that happened to you. Anything else happen to you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I been struck a couple times by the-- like yesterday, [INAUDIBLE], which my ear is almost broken. But I got good-- good sounds. Only I have noise in my head. But I got excellent sounds. I hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is still remaining with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got broken drum from-- from the hitting. Then all day, 24 hours, I got noise like a motor. Like you know while I talk, I hear the noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. But it doesn't interfere with your hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I get-- I get used to it. No, I got good hearing. I don't know, a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said also at Auschwitz, that you were taken for some experiments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick, I believe, 1942, in Auschwitz. Like, there was evening appell. All [INAUDIBLE] men forward, after-- after appell. So-- so-- ever-- whoever sick, or got [INAUDIBLE] on their foot, forward the-- for the checking.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- my captain-- like, the doctor. They ask me in Polish, do you have temperature? You have a-- they say Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Show me your tongue. I mean, [SPEAKING POLISH]. They speak beautiful Polish. Kept on like a-- a-- a doctor. Then they left, left me to [INAUDIBLE] Hospital. And many people wants to go too, into [INAUDIBLE], healthy people. All right, hold them. And so soon, eight SS truck-- army truck come in. And they get loaded in trucks, straight to crematorium, to gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Miracle. So I was very sick. I got a thing I'm going to show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh my goodness, on your leg. And what happen-- so they took you to the hospital there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. I had [INAUDIBLE] on both legs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm strong. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even every morning I made it for a little push up, even today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then too, you kept yourself going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in the hospital. Now, this was a hospital--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --only for the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Uh-huh. So next morning, the same day I was worried-- same-- same-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --date, same night, I was sick. I had water in-- I believe, in the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your lungs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had-- next morning, when I come in, they-- I don't know which doctor, or Dr-- Dr. Mengele, or the other doctor, two of them took me to the laboratorium next-- next day from-- from the [INAUDIBLE]. They drain the blood from my brain.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do it? Tell me what exactly-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was passed out. I was-- I know that both of them-- there was like-- like here, two-story building. The doctor, Mengele, or the other doctor, took me on the arm, both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the other doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have to find out in Auschwitz. Then they-- in a few second, I was dead. They brought me into Auschwitz. And the Dr. Mengele or the other doctor kept on-- told the-- the doctor prisoner. Because Doctor told me after that he was cured. Because they told me I was very lucky. And the doctor, the prisoner told me, you are very lucky. You know the doctor-- they kept on-- the doctor told us if I'm [INAUDIBLE], we-- we're going to [INAUDIBLE] for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wanted you to be taken care of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was-- I had very high fever. They tied me to the bed. I broke every piece of a blanket. I tried to jump through the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were afraid to stay in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I was a-- a high temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was like, you know, after that operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- you were taken. And was this the normal hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was this the hospital they did experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- no, no, it was a normal hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to you in the normal hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they-- that's-- like especially, I don't know why they was interested about me. Those-- those two and [INAUDIBLE] and a doctor, Dr. Mengele, or I don't know which-- which one. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like? Do you remember what he looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a top brass SS man, with a rank, medallion, nice, handsome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young man. That I must say, I was young too. Mengele was young too. I have-- I have his picture in the book. He was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1942?

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer? Winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe yes, in the fall, I believe, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Because that was raining, snow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they went to the regular hospital. And in the bed that you were staying in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they performed something on your-- on your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- they call me to the room. I woke up. And they took me on the arm, those two doctor, to them labratorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the barrack? Do you know where that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was especially that barrack for the-- for the-- for the-- for the doctor, the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to know the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, they stay-- you stayed there. Did you stay there for a period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I pass out. I don't know, nothing. I was dead. I was completely-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You still had this fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I had fever after that when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any mark on your head? Do you know where they worked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess-- I guess somewhere-- somewhere right here, they-- they cut me here, somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: This happened to you once, as you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just once.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they just stuck something into your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. the doctor told me I had like a stroke blood flooded my brain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- they were using you to test?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then after, that's why the doctor, the SS told the-- doctor prisoners, if I'm going to [? died, ?] all of you are going to [? died. ?] So take care of-- let me take care of him. And that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they wanted to use you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now they did this test, and you came back. You-- I mean, you-- whatever they did to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after that, they-- they ship out from Auschwitz to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple-- couple weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you stay inside that experimental barrack? Or did you go back to your old barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, I-- I really-- I guess I went normal, to the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were inside the barrack with the two doctors, I know you were sick. Do you remember anything about the inside? Can you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a main room, like this one. There was table. And-- and they just didn't-- didn't talk to me. They-- they talk between themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. They knew already, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full of medicines, full of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --instruments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and about the room, mirrors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other patients in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody but me. They took me. Doctor SS with his-- they're calling [GERMAN]. And because I was so weak I could not walk they took me on-- on the arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were the only person on this table?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And only the two doctors were in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you recovered from whatever they did, they sent you back to the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- you know, I don't know nothing. Because I was like a dead. They did something to me in the-- in the doctor office. Then I don't know how, I find myself in a-- in a bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In their barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were feeling all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember having any reaction afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a good memory, a good everything, a good spirit. Till now, I believe in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you feel that this experiment did not do anything to you negatively?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So far, till now, I'm just myself. I'm normal person. So maybe try to did some to me. And thank God, I'm the miracle one.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never touched you again? They didn't do anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --else to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after they ship out to finish us in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you say to finish you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when-- when they give us now a tattoo number.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- oh, after this experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you got a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In-- in Birkenau, they give us already.

speaker: interviewer
text: On-- your chest number is from Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's talk about your trip from Auschwitz to Birkenau. How did they take you to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe we had been in-- that-- that's not far away, about maybe five kilometer, maybe three kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe we walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe 10,000, maybe more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many people in Birkenau, by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, many people. They were so disorganized. They were-- the barracks was not ready. There-- there was no place to sleep. There were-- that was like chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep? Where did you end up sleeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: In different barracks. Because there was straw with the-- with the-- as you call it, mattress, what they fill it with straw. But also on the pile.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sleep where-- where-- whatever we can. Because it was-- for a couple days, it was disorganized. The SS don't know what to do. And that was unbelievable. So these were 1,000 people now, in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one else was there before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the first into Birkenau--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time? Now, the transports, when they came, do they come to Auschwitz? Or do they come to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, when the Birkenau build it, they come equal. Mostly transport from Europe come to [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was very famous, two crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when you got there, did they give you the number immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- maybe couple-- couple days or couple-- couple weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do it? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now there was each number, 3/4 needle. And they press it through the skin. You can hear when the-- when the skin was like, pop, pops. So many Russian soldier, prisoner been also tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many of them younger people, they fainted. When they-- when they're letting go from the war, they fainted. They could not stand it. To me, it was [INAUDIBLE]. They hurt me a little bit but I don't know, something. You can deal. Could I ask you to move your shirt just a little bit so we can take a picture of the number? Can you tell us the numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22, 54, [GERMAN]. German too, I will never forget it. Because I were what the German call. [GERMAN], what's your name? Number? [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: You no longer had a name. [LAUGHS] After the number, what did they do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, there was three-- three or four barrack where-- that-- that was in the fall, in-- in Birkenau. They closed down-- in the evening, barracks. I believe that was wintertime, because the frosty ground, in the snow on the ground, we have to take bath. And that-- this barrel was deep in the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the time I had big-- almost like I could see my bone. I have to jump into the-- into the-- the barrel and wash myself. And so the barracks was closed and nobody-- and nobody go into the barracks. And in the morning, half of those people been froze dead. You survived it. What was-- do you have-- did you have a number of the barrack in-- in Birkenau? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- really, I don't remember. That was not by number. But I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, when you were taking these 1,000 people to Birkenau, were there Jews mixed in with you? Or separate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that we been together with the Polish-Jewish, you know, Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said also, Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian soldiers at war. That's especially Russian-- Russian soldiers had number with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some Jewish people got numbers on their chest too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe at start of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these 1,000 were men. At that time, did you ever see any gypsies in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw-- I been-- after change commando, I'd been working as a [GERMAN]. Dug-- That mean repairing roof, put new roof on the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Birkenau. We went to the gypsy barracks. We went to the-- woman barracks, separated from the gypsy. Gypsy used to live in the Birkenau, whole family, with the children. With like 10, 12, 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they lived as families?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the family, yes. In the beginning, the German deliver them for the children, milk and white bread, by purpose. In a couple days, everybody go to gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1942?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just killed them en mass?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gypsies. Did you ever work in the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we been talking about gas chamber, crematorium. So to me, they was scared by even looking. At the nighttime, Birkenau, there was a-- a smoke come from a chimney with-- with the-- white [INAUDIBLE] spark. And-- and the smell, there was an odor from the-- from the bone. So it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you smelt this all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Daytime, nighttime. That crematorium was working 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet anybody that worked in the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I-- I spoke with the-- I believe it was not allowed, Polish-Jewish who work in it. They're calling sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: He talked about what his work was?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew what-- what going to happen to them. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did they-- what did they say about what was going to happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they knew. After a couple months I say, we have to go. They're going to pick up fresh people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did they-- were they strong-looking men, the people who worked in the sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice-looking people, young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they have more food because they-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. But they were some very young, nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear about an area that was called Kanada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kanada, and there was people who pick up-- yes, leave the jewelry, money on the side, the clothes on the other side. And that's called Kanada. In fact, in Kanada, they're calling. In Kanada, that was people will-- will pick-- pick up the clothes and jewelry, separate it. And I mean, many, many of them-- they-- they stole the jewelry, mostly the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a black market going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The black market was in Auschwitz, I'll tell you true. SS bring them whiskey, cold cuts, salami, and things like that. And they try to buy the jewelry, gold coins from-- from Polish prisoner in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see it in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe Birkenau. I-- but there was-- the Kanada was in Birkenau, in Auschwitz too. That this is the reason why they call-- called it Kanada. Because Kanada, there was everything, what you needed. What you collected, like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A pantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Gold, diamond, rubies, everything, everything. Mostly the people brought from France, from also, very rich people. And they have to leave everything. And-- and they get either way, gas chamber or going to-- to concentration camp, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able, ever, to work in these areas where they collected the materials like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no chance about here, about the Kanada, yes. That's the Kanada. Till now that Kanada, that's-- that's a goodie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you-- you never had the opportunity to get any goods or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But I know the people who work in-- in fact, in Kanada. People who work, in fact, in Kanada, people who give a new uniform for us, they-- they change the clothes maybe every couple weeks, fresh one. And Given a shirt. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to work in the kitchen so you would have some more food?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was sick after, I went to-- they're calling [INAUDIBLE]. They're peeling potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will discuss it on the next tape. [LAUGHS]
